coredata.dc:title: Pd-doped g-C3N4 decorated by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot as a high performance electrocatalyst with superior durability and methanol tolerance for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Faraji, Monireh
Moradi Dehaghi, Shahram
coredata.dc:description: Palladium doped graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (Pd-g-C3N4) decorated with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD) were fabricated as non-platinum nitrogen macrocyclic electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in alkaline media. The physical properties studies approved the formation of Pd-N. Moreover, it is confirmed that Pd-g-C3N4 has chemical interaction with NCQD. The electrochemical performance investigations showed that Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD majorly catalyzed ORR through four electron path. The enhancement of ORR kinetic is mainly associated with presence of the Pd-Nx reaction sites and rapid electron transfer occurring at the interface of Pd-g-C3N4 and NCQD. The prepared Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD presented comparable catalytic activity, outstanding methanol resistance and long lasting function period in comparison to commercial Pt/C. These properties could introduce it as a promising catalyst material for ORR in direct methanol fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen reduction
Graphitic carbon nitride
Methanol tolerance
Carbon quantum dot

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assembly of Nitrogen- doped carbon quantum dot (NCQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical presentation of Pd-g-C3N4 catalyst preparation route.
(a) Typical TEM images of g-C3N4 (b) Pd-g-C3N4 (c) N-CQDs on Pd-g-C3N4, (d) closer view and HRTEM image of Pd-g-C3N4 /NCQD.
(a) XRD patterns (b), FI-IR spectra and (c) Raman spectra (c) of g-C3N4, Pd-g-C3N4, NCQD, Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD.
(a) XPS spectrum of the prepared Pd-g-C3N4, survey spectra, (b) C 1 s spectra, (c) N 1 s spectra, (d) Pd 3d spectra.

(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K, and (b) Pore size distribution, calculated based on BJH model, of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD, Pd-g-C3N4, (c) Pore size distribution, calculated based on QSDFT model, of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD, Pd-g-C3N4 (d) Nyquist plot of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD, Pd-g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 at 0.3 V vs Ag/AgCl.

(a) Cv (scan rate 50 mV S-1) (b) LSV (scan rate 5 mV S-1 with 1500 rpm) of Pd-g-C3N4, Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and Pt/C (20%, ETEK) under O2 saturated atmosphere, (c) Tofel Plot of fabricated eletrodes, (d) LSV of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD at different rotating rates.

(a) and (b) accelerated test of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and Pt/C (20%, ETEK) in 0.1 M KOH, O2 saturated atmosphere with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 among −0.6 to 0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl for 10,000 cycles, (c) the alteration of relative current (J/J0) with time at −0.3 V for Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and Pt/C,(d) the chronoampermetry of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and Pt/C responses at −0.3 V in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte solution in the presence of 1 M CH3COH.
Calculated Electrochemical Parameters of Fabricated Electrodes.

head.ce:title: Pd-doped g-C3N4 decorated by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot as a high performance electrocatalyst with superior durability and methanol tolerance for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, P.O. Code 1651153311, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, P.O. Code 1651153311, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Palladium doped graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (Pd-g-C3N4) decorated with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD) were fabricated as non-platinum nitrogen macrocyclic electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in alkaline media. The physical properties studies approved the formation of Pd-N. Moreover, it is confirmed that Pd-g-C3N4 has chemical interaction with NCQD. The electrochemical performance investigations showed that Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD majorly catalyzed ORR through four electron path. The enhancement of ORR kinetic is mainly associated with presence of the Pd-Nx reaction sites and rapid electron transfer occurring at the interface of Pd-g-C3N4 and NCQD. The prepared Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD presented comparable catalytic activity, outstanding methanol resistance and long lasting function period in comparison to commercial Pt/C. These properties could introduce it as a promising catalyst material for ORR in direct methanol fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The ever-rising energy consumption followed by environmental eradication has sparked plenty of researches on green and renewable energy conversion. Proton exchange membrane fuel cells are deemed as one of the favorable energy convertors since they produce energy without any hazardous emission [1–6]. However, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), having struggling kinetics, is dealt with the impeding factors hinders the fuel cell commercialization. The efficacy of ORR completely depends on the performance of the used catalyst. Pt-based catalysts are known as the most effective electrocatalysts for ORR. However, high cost, scarcity, low stability and CO poisoning have limited their wide use. To address these issues, great effort has spent on designing non-Pt catalysts for ORR [7,8].

Novel group of non-platinum ORR catalysts such as doped carbon structures [9,10], metal oxides [11–14], nitrides[15,16], carbides[17–19] and transition metal chalgonides [20] have been introduced in recent years. Among them carbon based frameworks doped with nitrogen have caught much attention [21–23]. The large electronegativity of nitrogen is a decisive factor affecting the catalyst performance by imposing positive charge on neighboring carbon atoms.

The graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) possessing sp2-bonded C-N framework induces great physicochemical and thermal stability and proper electronic structure, has made it as a promising catalyst material for different range of chemical reactions such as photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, hydrogen storage and oxygen reduction reaction in Fuel Cells [24–31]. However, some weaknesses like low electrical conductivity, challenging syntheses process and low methanol tolerance are major barriers limiting its wide application in fuel cell electrodes. In contrast to mentioned drawbacks, the presence of plentiful melon moieties provide an ideal bedrock for the structural modification and binding with metal ions. Incorporation of metal ion to the structure of g-C3N4 could profoundly alter its electronic structure and introduce it as an efficient catalyst for various chemical reactions [25,32–36]. The enhanced catalytic and photocatalytic activity have been reported for numerous metal doped g-C3N4 structures [25,31,32,34,37,38]. However, the poor electron conductivity is the major barrier lowering the catalytic efficiency of g-C3N4 structures. Thus in this work carbon quantum dots have been used as a conductive platforms improving the charge transfer kinetics of Pd doped g-C3N4.Having superior methanol tolerance and suitable activity toward oxygen reduction, palladium based catalyst have extensively been investigated[39–43]. Incorporation of Pd atom into the g-C3N4 structure has been investigated as a potent and facile way which could modify the g-C3N4 [44]. In addition, merging carbon structure and g-C3N4 could result in the integration of their particular virtues with boosted or distinctive characteristics in the obtaining composites [38,45–47].

Therefore, the aim of the present work is the design of a high performance Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD nitrogen rich framework for ORR with enhanced methanol resistance and better durability. The fabricated Pd-g-C3N4 catalyst with Pd-Nx moiety serving as an active site presents a striking activity toward ORR in alkaline medium
with appropriate durability and proper methanol tolerance as compared with commercial Pt/C catalyst.

Specifically, 4 g of melamine was mixed with 50 mL of deionized water and stirred vigorously. After which, 0.1 g of PdCl2 was added to the prepared liquid. The suspension was heated to 85 °C. Heating was continued till deletion of water and this process followed by drying at 60 °C in vacuum oven. The obtained product was milled and annealed at 555 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h (with a constant heating rate of 5 °C min−1) in tube furnace. The acquired solid was milled and sieved with 400 mesh sieves to attain the Pd- g-C3N4. Moreover, pure g-C3N4 was made by the similar method in the absence of PdCl2 [31]. The used route for fabrication of the Pd-g-C3N4 catalysts is graphically demonstrated in Fig. 1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of Nitrogen- doped carbon quantum dot (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The NCQDs were fabricated through a hydrothermal method. Citric acid monohydrate was used as the carbon precursor, while L-cysteine as a source of nitrogen. In a typical procedure, 4 g of citric acid, and 0.1 g of L-cysteine were added to 20 mL of deionized water and kept under strong stir. After that, the obtained blend was treated through the hydrothermal rout at 250 °C for 12 h in a stainless steel autoclave. The resulting light yellow suspension was filtered and centrifuged at 20000 rpm for 30 min to eliminate remained large carbon particles. Then, the product was dialyzed with dialysis membrane for 12 h [48].

Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD composite were synthesized by a facile in-situ method. Briefly, 4mgL-1 of NCQD was mixed with melamine and PdCl2 solution under vigorous stirring for 2 h. , The mixture was heated in 100C until the evaporation of solvent. The resulted blend was heated to 555 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in a muffle furnace and kept at this temperature for 4 h. Reaching to the room temperature, the obtained Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD composite was milled into a fine powder.

Five working electrodes were fabricated by the mixing of 5 μL of the composites containing four prepared catalysts (Pd-g-C3N4, Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD, g-C3N4, NCQD) and commercial Pt/C with deionized water and 5% Nafion solution. The suspensions were sonicated and homogenized for about 45 min. Then a droplet of these suspensions was loaded on glassy carbon electrode with the surface area of 1 cm2.The containing solvent of electrodes evaporated in vacuum oven in 80⁰ C for 1 h. The amount of catalyst loaded on the glassy carbon electrodes were ~0.3 mg cm−2 [49]. For ORR performance analysis, the prepared electrodes were immersed in a 0.1 M KOH solution, employed as the electrolyte, and the rotating disk electrode studies were conducted in O2 saturated condition. Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat was used to carry out the electrochemical examinations such as (Cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), impedance spectroscopy, chronoamperometry)

The crystallographic structures of fabricated samples was acquired using X-ray Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer under copper Kα radiation. The morphology of synthesized catalyst were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (TEM-Philips). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, performed on Micromeritics ASAP 2504 N, was used to obtain the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms which is used for the surface calculation. To examine the surface chemical bonding and chemical states of the elements, the prepared catalysts, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by X-ray electron spectrometer microprobe using a radiation source of Al Kα (1486.6 eV) in an ultrahigh vacuum (UHV) system. To recognize the functional groups of the catalysts, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) studies were carried out on PerkinElmer FTIR spectrometer. Raman spectra was obtained by a 514 nm laser.

The as-prepared catalysts were undertaken to morphological investigations using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 2
a g-C3N4 illustrates plane but stacked layers with dense sheets resulted by the heat treatment at 555 °C for 2 h.; The Fig. 2 b presents that Pd nanoparticles are homogeneously dispersed on g-C3N4 nanosheets. The uniform structure of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD, seen in Fig. 2 c, could be considered as an evidence indicating that Pd are not found in the form of palladium oxide thus they are successfully incorporated into the g-C3N4 structures. Moreover, the wrapped structure in comparison to that in Fig. 2 a, proposes a chemically strong interlinkage between Pd doped g-C3N4 and N-CQDs instead of a simple physical mixing. This morphology suggests the formation of a vigorous electronic coupling at the interfaces of Pd-g-C3N4 and N-CQDs particles. The given TEM imagein Fig. 2d shows the spherical shape of the NCQDs with average diameter of ~5 nm which are homogeneously and densely dispersed in the fabricated sample. The provided inset HRTEM image illustrates two obvious crystal structure with marginal spacing of 0.22 nm attributed to the basal spacing of the (111) lattice plane of Pd and 0.24 nm assigned to interlayer spacing of the NCQDs that are entwined. NCQDs are dispersed among the Pd nanoparticles and a Pd-g-C3N4 and NCQD interface is formed. No severe agglomeration can be seen in the Pd-g-C3N4 /NCQD samples

Fig. 3
a shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of the fabricated g-C3N4, N-CQD, Pd-g-C3N4, Pd-g-C3N4/N-CQD composites. Regarding g-C3N4 XRD pattern, the diffraction peak at 23.8° is related to the close-knit stacking between the layers of the π-conjugate aromatic framework, which are indexed to the (002) planes with the strong interplanar binding distance of 0.315 nm, which is the distinctive characteristic of the melon system [50]. The other weaker diffraction peak at 9.57⁰ is attributed to the in-plane reflection, which is characteristically liable for the (100) diffraction planes related to ordering of tri-s-triazine structure units with distance of 0.655 nm [38].This is in agreement with the g-C3N4 structure described in former studies. There is no peak related to Pd, indicating that the Pd atoms accomplished to incorporate into the g-C3N4 structure. The broader peak suggests that after Pd doping into g-C3N4 matrix the resulted structure has lower crystalline structure and more edge defects .On the pattern of N-CQD, the broad peak at 21.41⁰ is revealing of (002) which is ascribed to carbon based materials [51]. The wide diffraction peak at 26.5⁰ observed for Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD shows a layer-to-layer interspace (d = 0.350 nm) between Pd-g-C3N4 and N-CQD, specifying that NCQD is properly distributed on Pd-g-C3N4.

Considering the FT-IR spectra, seen in Fig. 3 b, it can be inferred that the pristine C-N graphitic nature remains almost entirely unaffected after the incorporation of carbon quantum dots and Pd ions. The peak observed at 808.5 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibration of s-triazine units. The intensity of this band for Pd-g-C3N4/ NCQD is lower than that of g-C3N4 which is related to the breathing mode of s-triazine rings. In addition, the absorption bands seen at 1210–1640 cm−1 area are responsible for stretching mode of C = N and C-N in the triazine ring. These bands are similarly observed on the spectra of Pd-g-C3N4/ NCQD. The peaks, which are seen in area > 3000 cm−1,are attributed to the N-H stretching vibration [31]. For NCQDs, a broad absorption bands is observed in 3100–360000 cm−1 which is the characteristic of O–H and N-H vibrational starching [52].

Fig. 3 c illustrates the Raman spectra of the fabricated pristine g-C3N4 and Pd-doped g-C3N4/NCQD. The raman spectrum of g-C3N4 nanosheets shows two strong band in 765 and 1289 cm-1attributed to breathing modes of the s-triazine rings and stretching vibration of C-N and C = N bonds, respectively,[53]. It could be obviously observed that the pd-g-C3N4/NCQD nanosheets displays similar behavior as those of g-C3N4. However, the peaks for pd-g-C3N4/NCQD demonstrate slight shift to upper wave numbers indicating smaller crystalline size of pd-g-C3N4/NCQD.

To study the surface chemical composition of different species, the X-ray photoemission spectroscopic (XPS) spectrum of Pd-g-C3N4 was obtained. As seen in Fig. 4
a the prepared sample contains C, N, O, and Pd elements presenting strong peaks in survey scan XPS spectra. The weak peaks of O may be attributed to absorbed O2 or H2O on the surface of Pd-g-C3N4 [54]. The C 1 s XPS spectra is depicted in Fig. 4 b. There can be found a broad peak at 288.2 eV, which is deconvoluted into two peaks placed at 287.3 eV and 288.4 eV. These peaks represent the N-C = N bonds in the aromatic framework and the sp2-bonded carbon grafted to NH2 groups, respectively [55]. The weak peak positioned at 283.5 eV is related to sp2 C = C bonds, stemming from partly and imperfectly polymerization of the precursor [56]. At the same time in Fig. 4 c, the deconvoluted N 1 s spectra of Pd-g-C3N4 shows three distinctive peaks situated at 399.2 eV, 400.8 eV, and 404.2 eV.The principal peak located at 399.2 eV is attributed to pyridinic-N (C-N = C) in the triazine rings and the peak positioned at 400.8 eV is ascribed to tertiary N-(C)3 [57]. In addition, it is worth mentioning that the C 1 s, N 1 s spectrum of Pd-g-C3N4 are observed in higher binding energies than those of g-C3N4, proposing the generation of Pd-N coordinate bond. Specifically, the unoccupied molecular orbital is provided by Pd atom for bonding, whereas the lone pair electrons are donated by N atom in CN aromatic ring. Subsequently, the electron density of N atom was lessened, that also leads to the decline of that of C atom. Consequently, the binding abilities of the nucleus were enhanced for the electron, bringing the rise of the orbital of C 1 s and N 1 s for Pd-g-C3N4 structure. Considering the Pd 3d XPS spectra (Fig. 4 d), the peaks placed at 335.5 and 229.2 eV which could be deconvoluted into peaks located at 333.2, 334.1, 338.1, and 338.8 eV, are ascribed to PdO and Pd0, respectively.

With the aim of additional identification of the porous structure and specific surface area of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and g-C3N4, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were defined by employing multilayer Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) adsorption–desorption theory. Fig. 5
a displays the typical IV isotherms of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and g-C3N4 suggesting mesoporous frameworks. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of the Pd-g C3N4/NCQD and g-C3N4 are measured to be 150 m2g−1 and 105 with a pore volume of 0.40 cm3g−1 and 0.15 denoting. The alteration in the surface properties evidently shows the incorporation of Pd atoms into the g-C3N4 sheets. Using Barrett–Joyer–Halenda (BJH) model, the average pore size of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and g-C3N4 were calculated 4 and 15 nm, respectively, proving the well spreading of the Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD pores. The presence of mesopors could be helpful for ORR reaction. Moreover, The pore size distribution analysis calculated based on QSDFT shows that the pore radius range is approximately 2–10 nm. The obtained results from QSDFT model are in proper accordance with that of BJH method.

In attempts to find out about the electrical conductivity of prepared catalysts, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out. The related Nyquist plots is seen in Fig. 5 c. Being accountable for the charge transfer resistance, high frequency region presents a semicircle which could be used to obtain the charge transfer value. The obtained charge transfer resistance values for Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD, Pd-g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 are 548, 636 and 1100 Ω, respectively. These results indicate the faster charge transfer kinetic in Pd-gC3N4/NCQD rooted in the Pd doping and the presence of NCQD remarkably rising the electrical conductivity of g-C3N4.

The electrocatalytic performance of prepared elecatalysts toward ORR were inspected by means of RDE. The obtained CV curves, Fig. 6
a, demonstrate that in contrast to NCQD and g-C3N4, Pd-g-C3N4 and Pd-g C3N4/NCQD present obvious ORR peaks. The distinct ORR peak in O2 with a peak potential of −62 mV is observed for Pd-g C3N4/NCQD, which is marginally negative than that for Pt/C (-50 mV).As shown in Fig. 6 b and Table 1 , all electrochemical parameters of Pd-g C3N4/NCQD obtained from the LSV plot present remarkable enhancement.

Despite the fact that the Pt/C (20%, ETEK) demonstrates a better performance than the Pd-g C3N4/NCQD catalyst, the obtained performance parameters for Pd-g C3N4/NCQD, seen in Table 1, are completely comparable with those of commercial Pt/C. These results suggest that Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD could be utilized as a high performance, low-cost and non-platinum ORR electrocatalyst. This resulted improvement could be attributed to Pd doping in the g-C3N4 which may act as a component facilitating the electron-transfer processes through the reaction. Moreover, the higher activity of Pd-g C3N4/NCQD than Pd-g-C3N4 indicates that the presence of NCQD in catalyst structure has synergistic effect. This is due to the fact that the NCQD particles are grafted to the surface of Pd-g C3N4 to rise the active sites and effective O2 transfer. In addition, the Tafel plots seen in Fig. 6 c indicates that Pd-g C3N4/NCQD shows a smaller slope (43 mV dec-1) than does Pd-g-C3N4 (47 mV dec-1), which is in accordance with its faster kinetic. This finding is confirmed by impedance date, presented in Fig. 5 c, implying that Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD has smaller charge transfer resistance than does Pd-g-C3N4. Besides the Tafel slope of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD (43 mV per dec-1) is almost equal to the values of the Pt/C catalysts (40 mV per dec-1), representing approximately the matching ORR mechanism of the Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and Pt/C.

So as to study the electron transfer mechanism, a chain of LSVs at various electrode rotation rates for Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD, Pd-g-C3N4, and Pt/C, (Fig. 1S), are acquired to calculate the number of transferred electrons (n) and kinetic-limiting current density (Jk) by using the Koutechy-Levich equation [88]. Based on the Koutecky-Levich curves, the number of electrons transferred on the Pd-g-C3N4, Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and Pt/C catalysts were calculated 3.5, 4.05, 4.2, respectively, signifying that the four-electrons transfer route is used by Pd-g-C3N4, Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and Pt/C (20%, ETEK) catalysts to perform the ORR. The higher electron transfer number of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD than Pd-g-C3N4 suggests that NCQD is also active in ORR

process. The porous structure of g-C3N4 facilitating the catalyst particle interaction and reactants increases the mass transfer kinetics. The presence of N-doped carbon quantum dots in catalyst structure significantly raises the electrical conductivity of composite resulting in lower kinetic polarization rooted in low electron transfer rate [58].

Even though some investigated non-platinum metal catalysts exhibit fairly high ORR performance, stability is of great importance for practical applications. The longterm stability Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD of and commercial Pt/C is evaluated by performing the chronoamperometric analysis at 0.3 V for 10 h. Moreover, The accelerated degradation test (ADT) was carried out in under O2 satuarated atmosphere

0.1 M KOH with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 among −0.6 to 0.2 V vs Ad/AgCl for 10,000 cycles. As seen in Fig. 7
a, the LSV curves for both Pd-g C3N4/NCQD, are almost overlay before and after ADT, showing the greater durability comparedwith commercial Pt/C, presenting a noticeable decay. The evident degradation of Pt/C (remarkable decline of onset potential and E1/2, but no apparent decay in jL) is mostly attributed to the leaching of Pt nanoparticles at high potentials. The outstanding durability of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD complex originates not only from high resistance of g-C3N4 catalyst and NCQD against corrosion but also from the intimate interaction between Pd-g-C3N4 and NCQD. These obtained results further confirmed with constant chronoamperometric studies ( Fig. 7 c). After the 10 h reaction time, the observed percentage of performance loss is about 40% whereas, the current density of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD catalyst presented insignificant activity loss (about 10.5%).

Methanol crossover from the anode to cathode could be considered as one of the main factors leading to the performance loss in DMFC fuel cells. An ideal electrocatalyst for cathode in direct methanol fuel cells ought to exhibit superior activity toward oxygen reduction and high methanol resistance. Being tolerant toward methanol cross over, Pd is used as the main catalyst component. As well as high methanol resistance, Pd based electrocatalysts have shown superior catalytic activity toward ORR [59]. To evaluate the tolerance of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD and Pt/C to methanol poisoning, 2 mL of 1 M methanol solution was added to electrolyte during the ORR chronoampermetry measurments. As seen in Fig. 7 d, while the Pt/C catalyst experiences about 3.5% immediate drop in cathodic current after the interruption with methanol solution, the observed current loss for Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD is negligible, suggesting that the prepared catalyst has great methanol tolerance. The outstanding methanol tolerance capability of Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD introduces its possible application as a non-platinum electroctlyst for DMFC in alkaline medium.

In this work we have fabricated Palladium doped graphitic carbon nitride catalyst (Pd-g-C3N4) decorated with nitrogen doped carbon quantum dot (NCQD). Compared to Pt/C (20%, ETEK), the prepared catalyst exhibits great performance toward ORR, along with enduring durability and methanol tolerance. This indicates that the as-synthesized two dimensional Pd-g-C3N4/NCQD could be used as a promising non-platinum catalyst for ORR in fuel cells. The observed improvement of ORR performance is attributed to the Pd-Nx structures, which could be deemed as active sites for ORR. The fast charge transfer kinetic is rooted in the interaction between the NCQD and Pd-g-C3N4. Consequently, in this work, the incorporation of Pd into the g-C3N4 structure and its composition with NCQD has been introduced as an efficient strategy enhancing the ORR catalytic performance.

All persons who meet authorship criteria are listed as authors, and all authors certify that they have participated sufficiently in the work to take public responsibility for the content, including participation in the concept, design, analysis, writing, or revision of the manuscript. Furthermore, each author certifies that this material or similar material has not been and will not be submitted to or published in any other publication before its appearance in the inorganic chemistry communication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2020.108328.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
